{'text': '岐阜県加茂郡富加町大山', 'language': 'ja'} [[JA:大山白山神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11434667}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine | Name = Oyama Hakusan Shrine | Image = [[File:Oyama hakusan shrine.jpg|250px|Oyama Hakusan Shrine]]
Shrine buildings | Location = {{ill|lt=Gifu Prefecture|Q131277|WD=Q131277|1=Gifu Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Kamo District|Q1348656|WD=Q1348656|1=Kamo District, Gifu}}{{ill|lt=Shirakawa Town|Q1203350|WD=Q1203350|1=Shirakawa, Gifu (town)}} Mitono 1096 | Coordinates = {{Wiki coordinates 2-step degrees minutes seconds|35|37|10.41|N|137|13|40.00|E|scale:20000}} | Enshrined deities = {{ill|lt=Kikuri-hime no Kami|Q11619891|WD=Q11619891|1=Kukurihime}}　{{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813858|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi-no-Mikoto|1=Izanagi}}　{{ill|lt=Izanami|Q682306|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami-no-Mikoto|1=Izanami}} | Shrine rank = Former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}, disputed {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, {{ill|lt=Kinpei-sha|Q119929592|WD=Q119929592|1=Kinpei-sha}} | Founded = (Traditionally) Year 2 of {{ill|lt=Yōrō|Q1373061|WD=Q1373061|1=Yōrō}} ({{ill|lt=718|Q23534|WD=Q23534|1=718}}) | Main hall = | Annual festivals = {{ill|lt=Vernal Equinox Day|Q1049141|WD=Q1049141|1=Vernal Equinox Day}} (Spring Grand Festival)　November 23 (Autumn Grand Festival) | Rituals = | Map = {{Location map|Japan Gifu|label=Oyama
Hakusan Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=230}} }} '''Oyama Hakusan Shrine''' (おおやまはくさんじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|lt=Shirakawa Town|Q1203350|WD=Q1203350|1=Shirakawa, Gifu (town)}}, {{ill|lt=Kamo District|Q1348656|WD=Q1348656|1=Kamo District, Gifu}}, {{ill|lt=Gifu Prefecture|Q131277|WD=Q131277|1=Gifu Prefecture}}. It stands on the summit of Mount Hakusan (elevation 862 m). Its former {{ill|lt=shrine rank|Q10444029|WD=Q10444029|1=Shakaku}} was Prefectural Shrine. There is a theory identifying it with the Oyama Shrine of Kamo District in {{ill|lt=Mino Province|Q864156|WD=Q864156|1=Mino Province}} listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}. The current shrine buildings were rebuilt after the {{ill|lt=Kansei era|Q1195131|WD=Q1195131|1=Kansei}}. === Enshrined Deities === === Principal Deities === * {{ill|lt=Kikuri-hime no Kami|Q11619891|WD=Q11619891|1=Kukurihime}} (Hakusan Hime no Kami / {{ill|lt=Hakusan Gongen|Q11579578|WD=Q11579578|1=Hakusan Gongen}}) * {{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813858|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi-no-Mikoto|1=Izanagi}} * {{ill|lt=Izanami|Q682306|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami-no-Mikoto|1=Izanami}} === Deities of Subsidiary Shrines === * {{ill|lt=Kumano Gongen|Q11568925|WD=Q11568925|lt=Ketsumiko no Kami|1=Kumano Gongen}} * {{ill|lt=Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Q335618|WD=Q335618|lt=Hayatamao no Kami|1=Kumano Hayatama Taisha}} * {{ill|lt=Kumano Gongen|Q11568925|WD=Q11568925|lt=Kumano Fusumi no Kami|1=Kumano Gongen}} * {{ill|lt=Shinatsuhiko|Q1073925|WD=Q1073925|lt=Shinatsuhiko no Kami|1=Shinatsuhiko}} === Annual Festivals === === Spring Grand Festival === Held on {{ill|lt=Vernal Equinox Day|Q1049141|WD=Q1049141|1=Vernal Equinox Day}} in March. During the Spring Grand Festival, an offering envoy is dispatched from the Gifu Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines. After the ritual prayerA prayer recited by the offering envoy at regular festivals, enshrinement festivals, main hall relocation ceremonies, and periodic festivals at shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines. is offered, shrine maidens perform the Otome no Mai and Urayasu no Mai, and a lion dance (Shishi Kagura) is dedicated. === Autumn Grand Festival (Niiname-sai) === Held on {{ill|lt=Labor Thanksgiving Day|Q82405|WD=Q82405|1=Labor Thanksgiving Day}} in November. After the ritual prayer is recited, shrine maidens perform the Otome no Mai, Urayasu no Mai, and Shishi Kagura, and mochi-throwing is also held outside the worship hall. On the same day, {{ill|lt=Shichi-Go-San|Q1072387|WD=Q1072387|1=Shichi-Go-San}} prayers are also conducted. === History === According to tradition, in the 2nd year of {{ill|lt=Yōrō|Q1373061|WD=Q1373061|1=Yōrō}} ({{ill|lt=718|Q23534|WD=Q23534|1=718}}), {{ill|lt=Taichō|Q2388439|WD=Q2388439|1=Taichō}} transferred the deity from {{ill|lt=Hakusan Hime Shrine|Q704702|WD=Q704702|1=Shirayama Hime Shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Kaga Province|Q907469|WD=Q907469|1=Kaga Province}} and founded the shrine. One theory holds that it was one of the “Seven Hakusan Shrines of Hokuriku” established by Taichō in the same yearAnother of the Seven Hakusan Shrines of Hokuriku is {{ill|lt=Nōgō Hakusan Shrine|Q11612076|WD=Q11612076|1=Nōgō Hakusan Shrine}}.. It is said that when the deity was transferred, it was first enshrined via Mount Suishō (a 687 m mountain near the borders of Shirakawa Town, {{ill|lt=Hichisō Town|Q1203839|WD=Q1203839|1=Hichisō, Gifu}}, and {{ill|lt=Gero City|Q819639|WD=Q819639|1=Gero, Gifu}}). Since ancient times, it served as the chief guardian shrine of Shirakawa and Sami-go, with residents of 36 nearby villages as parishioners. In the 12th year of {{ill|lt=Bunmei (Japan)|Q1193544|WD=Q1193544|lt=Bunmei|1=Bunmei}} ({{ill|lt=1480|Q6628|WD=Q6628|1=1480}}), it was restored by '''Yasue Nakatsukasa-no-jō Mitsuami Motomasa''', lord of Nohara Castle. From the medieval period, the syncretic Shinto-Buddhist temple {{ill|lt=Muromatsu-ji (Shirakawa Town, Gifu Prefecture)|UNKNOWN|lt=Muromatsu-ji}} of {{ill|lt=Shugendō|Q1483875|WD=Q1483875|1=Shugendō}} served as the {{ill|lt=bettō-ji|Q11396755|WD=Q11396755|1=Bettō-ji}} (administrative temple) and conducted rituals. According to a provenance record of '''Oyama Hakusan Gongen''' dated September 18, the 6th year of {{ill|lt=Tenbun era|Q1192853|WD=Q1192853|lt=Tenbun|1=Tenbun}} ({{ill|lt=1537|Q6461|WD=Q6461|1=1537}}), signed and sealed by the Muromatsu-ji bettō '''Shamon Enkai''': {{Quote|The main hall represents the true Buddhist nature of Hakusan, the Eleven-faced Kannon, and is eleven bays square. The five principal icons in the inner sanctuary are: the central icon, {{ill|lt=Eleven-faced Kannon|Q11404731|WD=Q11404731|1=Ekādaśamukha}}; to the left and right, {{ill|lt=Thousand-armed Kannon|Q10904910|WD=Q10904910|1=Thousand-armed Kannon}}; the teacher of the Western Paradise, {{ill|lt=Maitreya|UNKNOWN|lt=Miroku Buddha}}; and the savior of the six realms, {{ill|lt=Jizō Bosatsu|Q604687|WD=Q604687|1=Kṣitigarbha}}. All of these were made by the Great Master Taichō. In the outer sanctuary are placed {{ill|lt=Fudō Myōō|UNKNOWN|lt=Fudō}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Bishamonten|1=UNKNOWN}}. Ascending to the inner sanctuary of Ōmine, there are three shrines: the central one is Hakusan Gongen, and the two side subsidiary shrines are Kumano and {{ill|lt=Suwa Shrine|Q11556504|WD=Q11556504|lt=Suwa Gongen|1=Suhara Shrine}}. Also, in the treasure hall {{ill|lt=Daikokuten|Q1759916|WD=Q1759916|1=Daikokuten}} is enshrined. Next, the principal icon of the Goma Hall of the bettō temple Muromatsu-ji is called the principal icon of Mount Betsu, a seated statue of Shō-Kannon Bosatsu, over four shaku in height, made by {{ill|lt=Gyōki|Q1136664|WD=Q1136664|1=Gyōki}} Bosatsu, the founder of this temple.}} It is noted at the end that although such origins of the mountain had existed since ancient times, some portions had become illegible due to insect damage. In August of the 4th year of {{ill|lt=Genki|Q1145064|WD=Q1145064|1=Genki (era)}} (the 1st year of {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}}) ({{ill|lt=1573|Q6678|WD=Q6678|1=1573}}), Nohara Castle fell to {{ill|lt=Tōyama Tomotada|Q11642011|WD=Q11642011|1=Tōyama Tomotada}}. The castle lord '''Yasue Sanai Masatomo''' fled to {{ill|lt=Bingo Province|Q1049113|WD=Q1049113|1=Bingo Province}}, while his younger brother '''Yasue Koyoemon Masanori''' eventually fled to {{ill|lt=Bungo Province|Q864148|WD=Q864148|1=Bungo Province}}, and their local power base was lost. In the 10th year of {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} ({{ill|lt=1582|Q6719|WD=Q6719|1=1582}}), Nohara Castle and Koshihara Castle fell after an attack by {{ill|lt=Mori Nagayoshi|Q1071391|WD=Q1071391|1=Mori Nagayoshi}}, lord of {{ill|lt=Kanayama Castle (Mino Province)|Q11646975|WD=Q11646975|lt=Kanayama Castle|1=Kaneyama Castle}}. The {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yasue clan|1=UNKNOWN}} thereafter abandoned the warrior class and returned to farming; others served the {{ill|lt=Asano clan|Q2659895|WD=Q2659895|1=Asano clan}} or became rural samurai. From then on, generation after generation, the Yasue clan took responsibility for the repair of Oyama Hakusan Shrine. During the Edo period, the area belonged to {{ill|lt=Naegi Domain|Q10863458|WD=Q10863458|1=Naegi Domain}}, but because parishioner villages were also under {{ill|lt=Tenryō|Q1685411|WD=Q1685411|lt=shogunate lands|1=Tenryō}} and {{ill|lt=Owari Domain|Q1062072|WD=Q1062072|1=Owari Domain}}, rice and money were donated by each magistrate whenever repairs were carried out. On September 15, the 1st year of {{ill|lt=Kan’en|Q1145723|WD=Q1145723|1=Kan'en}} ({{ill|lt=1748|Q7600|WD=Q7600|1=1748}}), the administrative head of {{ill|lt=Sanbō-in|Q3471686|WD=Q3471686|1=Sanbō-in}} in Kyoto Daigo granted the divine rank of {{ill|lt=Senior First Rank|Q11123258|WD=Q11123258|1=Senior First Rank}} to “Mino Province Oyama Hakusan Daigongen.” The construction from the 5th year of {{ill|lt=Kansei|Q1195131|WD=Q1195131|1=Kansei}} ({{ill|lt=1793|Q7807|WD=Q7807|1=1793}}) to the 12th year ({{ill|lt=1800|Q6875|WD=Q6875|1=1800}}), which took eight years, was the largest in scale and involved enormous expense. Because all residents of the foothill villages were parishioners, they cooperated across shogunate, Owari Domain, and Naegi Domain territories to contribute to the construction. The most important site supervisor was '''Takagi Kasuke''', headman of Izumi Village, and the chief accountant was '''Sugiyama Tasaburō''', headman of Yui Village. Donations from the rulers of the parishioner villages were as follows.{{Quotation|One: 50 bales of rice — Lord of Naegi Castle, {{ill|lt=Tōyama Tomohisa|Q11642009|WD=Q11642009|lt=Tōyama Sakichi|1=Tōyama Tomohisa}} One: 200 hiki of gold — {{ill|lt=Hida district magistrate|Q11665921|WD=Q11665921|1=Hida gundai}} Iizuka Ihe’e One: 200 hiki of gold — Owari {{ill|lt=Ōta Magistrate’s Office|Q109366193|WD=Q109366193|lt=Ōta magistrate|1=Ōta magistrate}} Nagasaka Ogisuke One: 200 hiki of gold — Owari {{ill|lt=Nishikori River Timber Magistrate’s Office|Q132154387|WD=Q132154387|lt=Nishikori magistrate|1=Nishikori magistrate}} Yao Kichisuke}} In the 4th year of {{ill|lt=Keiō|Q776775|WD=Q776775|1=Keiō}} ({{ill|lt=1868|Q7717|WD=Q7717|1=1868}}), with the enforcement of the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shinbutsu Bunri Edict|1=UNKNOWN}}, the bettō temple Muromatsu-ji was abolished and demolished. The bettō '''Eshitsu''' returned to lay life, changed his name to '''Muromatsu Mino (Muromatsu Yoshio)''', became a Shinto priest, and thereafter devoted himself to the promotion of Shinto. Also, a directive from {{ill|lt=Kasamatsu Prefecture|Q11599927|WD=Q11599927|1=Kasamatsu Prefecture}} noted the discrepancy in the shrine’s name “Mino Province Oyama Hakusan Daigongen,” and it was renamed Hakusan Shrine. The plaque reading “Mino Province Oyama Hakusan Daigongen” is currently preserved in the treasure storehouse. In the 6th year of Meiji ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}), it became a {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} for the Nishi-Shirakawa and Shimo-Sami districts. In the 15th year of Meiji ({{ill|lt=1882|Q7812|WD=Q7812|1=1882}}), the Yasue Kōan Shrine, enshrining Yasue Motomasa, was established as a precinct shrine. In the 39th year of Meiji ({{ill|lt=1906|Q2049|WD=Q2049|1=1906}}), the government implemented the shrine consolidation policy of one shrine per village, and subsidiary shrines were gathered within the precincts. In the 7th year of {{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}} ({{ill|lt=1918|Q2094|WD=Q2094|1=1918}}), it became a Prefectural Shrine. Thereafter, it was designated by the Governor of Gifu Prefecture as a {{ill|lt=shinsen-heihaku-ryō kyōshinsha|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=shrine receiving offerings of sacred food and silk|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} and was revered as a shrine protecting martial fortune. On March 1, {{ill|lt=1953|Q18585|WD=Q18585|1=1953}} (Shōwa 28), it was designated by the Gifu Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines as a Prefectural Shrine Association Head’s Visitation Shrine ({{ill|lt=Kinpei-sha|Q119929592|WD=Q119929592|1=Kinpei-sha}}) (Designation No. 19) {{sfn|Kimura|1997|p=123}}. === Painted Ceiling === In the 11th year of {{ill|lt=Kansei|Q1195131|WD=Q1195131|1=Kansei}} ({{ill|lt=1799|Q7827|WD=Q7827|1=1799}}), 32 Japanese-style color paintings of flowers, trees, animals, and people, painted by the young monk '''Mayuge''' of {{ill|lt=Dōun-ji (Shirakawa Town, Gifu Prefecture)|Q11555882|WD=Q11555882|lt=Dōun-ji|1=Tō'un-ji Temple}}, were affixed to the coffered ceiling of the main hall. They are famous as the “Painted Ceiling of Hakusan Shrine.” Mayuge is said to have been a genius not only in calligraphy and painting but also in scholarship. He moved to Edo to train at Kichijō-ji, but it is said that he was poisoned to death out of jealousy by his peers due to his extraordinary talent. === Precinct Shrines === === Yasue Kōan Shrine === The precinct shrine is separate from the subsidiary shrines. Around the time of the {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707|1=Meiji Restoration}}, there was a village group leader in Utsuo Village named '''Yasue Tarōshichi'''. Tarōshichi’s political career ended in the 6th year of Meiji ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}), and as a remembrance in his later years, he conceived the founding of Yasue Kōan Shrine, traveling far and wide. In the 15th year of Meiji ({{ill|lt=1882|Q7812|WD=Q7812|1=1882}}), the shrine was established within the precincts, enshrining Yasue Nakatsukasa-no-jō Mitsuami Motomasa as its deity. Tarōshichi’s diary contains the following entry: {{Quotation|One: Kamo District, Nohara Castle, Yasue Nakatsukasa-no-jō, Taira no Motomasa, during the Bunmei era, restored the Shirakawa-go village shrine Hakusan Shrine; by the 18th year of Meiji, approximately over 400 years had passed. Accordingly, the hall of Yasue Motomasa was rebuilt and his divine spirit enshrined. November 22, Meiji 18 Shirakawa Village — Organizer: Yasue Tarōshichi Mitono Village — Organizer: Carpenter Yasue Tannemon Shirakawa Village, Muwata — Yasue Tahei Shirakawa Village, Yui — Yasue Yasushi One: 24 yen 35 sen — Hall construction expenses One: 29 yen 35 sen 2 rin — Festival-related expenses One: 3 yen — Fence expenses Total: 56 yen 70 sen 2 rin Of which: 39 yen 21 sen rin — Voluntary donations 3 yen 53 sen 6 rin — Yasue Tahei 7 yen 7 sen 4 rin — Yasue Tarōshichi 2 yen 50 sen — Yasue Yasushi 1 yen — Yasue Tannemon 50 sen — Izumi, Yasue Kitarō 50 sen — Arimoto, Yasue Yūtarō 2 yen 33 sen 6 rin — Yasue Tarōshichi (Value of white rice: 4 to 9 shō 3 gō)}} === Designated Cultural Properties === === Nationally Designated Natural Monument === * Great Cedar of Oyama (National {{ill|lt=Natural Monument|Q23790|WD=Q23790|1=Natural monument}}){{Cite web|Japanese|date= |url=http://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazai/17768/tennen/ooyama.html |title=Great Cedar of Oyama |publisher=Gifu Prefecture |accessdate=2017-09-17}} On the right side of the worship hall, midway up the stone steps leading to the main hall, stands a giant cedar designated as a National Natural Monument, said to be over 1,200 years old, with a circumference of 8.9 m and a height of 36 m. Because the area around the shrine forms a well-preserved green space mainly consisting of {{ill|lt=Japanese cedar|Q147388|WD=Q147388|1=Cryptomeria}} and {{ill|lt=Japanese cypress|Q917187|WD=Q917187|1=Chamaecyparis obtusa}}, it was designated as a Green Environment Conservation Area on November 6, {{ill|lt=1984|Q2432|WD=Q2432|1=1984}} (Shōwa 59){{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/page/6062.html|title=List of Green Environment Conservation Areas|publisher=Gifu Prefecture|date=|accessdate=2024-10-20}}. === Access === ; Public Transportation * {{ill|lt=Central Japan Railway Company|Q513679|WD=Q513679|lt=JR|1=Central Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|lt=Takayama Main Line|Q1188357|WD=Q1188357|1=Takayama Main Line}} — Get off at {{ill|lt=Shirakawa-guchi Station|Q5358687|WD=Q5358687|1=Shirakawaguchi Station}} or {{ill|lt=Shimoyui Station|Q7497027|WD=Q7497027|1=Shimoyui Station}}. Approximately 15 km from Shirakawa-guchi Station, and about 10 km from Shimoyui Station. === Notes === {{Footnote help}} {{Reflist}} === References === * “Shirakawa Town History,” Volume 3: Life and Culture, Chapter 5: Religion, Section 2: Shrines Enshrined in Various Areas, 1. Former Nishi-Shirakawa Village, Mitono aza Oyama, Hakusan Shrine, pp. 839–842, Shirakawa Town History Editorial Committee, 1968 * “Shirakawa Town History,” Volume 3: Life and Culture, Chapter 5: Religion, Section 4: Temples, 2. Former Nishi-Shirakawa Village, Kyoto Sanbō-in branch temple, Shirakawa-san Muromatsu-ji, pp. 866–870, Shirakawa Town History Editorial Committee, 1968 * {{Cite book |Japanese |editor=Kimura Akira |title=Tour of Major Shrines of Gifu Prefecture |publisher={{ill|lt=Nagara Tenjin Shrine|Q3335060|WD=Q3335060|1=Nagara Tenjin Shrine}} |date=1997 |ref={{SfnRef|Kimura|1997}}}} === External Links === * [https://www.gifu-jinjacho.jp/syosai.php?shrno=495&shrname=%E2%98%85%E5%A4%A7%E5%B1%B1%E7%99%BD%E5%B1%B1%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE%E2%98%85 Oyama Hakusan Shrine – Gifu Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines] * [https://mino-shirakawa.com/explore/ooyamahakusannjinjya/ Oyama Hakusan Shrine – Shirakawa Town Tourism Association] * [https://mino-shirakawa.com/event/ooyamahakusannjinnjyasyunnkitaisai/ Oyama Hakusan Shrine Spring Grand Festival – Shirakawa Town Tourism Association] * [https://mino-shirakawa.com/event/ooyamahakusaooyamahakusannjinjya/ Oyama Hakusan Shrine Autumn Grand Festival (Niiname-sai) – Shirakawa Town Tourism Association] * [https://mino-shirakawa.com/explore/ooyamahakusanjinjyaoosugi/ Oyama Hakusan Shrine Great Cedar – Shirakawa Town Tourism Association] == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Ōyama Hakusan Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Ōyama Hakusan Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Mino Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Gifu Prefecture]] [[Category:白川町の建築物]] [[Category:白川町の歴史]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Mino Province]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Gifu Prefecture]] [[Category:白山神社]]

It is located in [[Mino Province]]. == instance of (P31) == * {{ill|Shinto shrine|lt=Shinto shrine|ar|جينجا|arz|ضريح شنتو|bcl|Shinto shrine|ca|Jinja|cs|Šintoistická svatyně|da|Shinto-tempel|de|Shintō-Schrein|el|Σιντοϊστικό Ιερό|en|Shinto shrine|eo|Ŝintoa sanktejo|es|Jinja|eu|Jinja|fa|معبد شینتویی|fi|Šintolainen pyhäkkö|fr|Sanctuaire shinto|hi|शिंतो {'text': '岐阜県加茂郡富加町大山', 'language': 'ja'} [[JA:大山白山神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11434667}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine | Name = Oyama Hakusan Shrine | Image = [[File:Oyama hakusan shrine.jpg|250px|Oyama Hakusan Shrine]]
Shrine buildings | Location = {{ill|lt=Gifu Prefecture|Q131277|WD=Q131277|1=Gifu Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Kamo District|Q1348656|WD=Q1348656|1=Kamo District, Gifu}}{{ill|lt=Shirakawa Town|Q1203350|WD=Q1203350|1=Shirakawa, Gifu (town)}} Mitono 1096 | Coordinates = {{Wiki coordinates 2-step degrees minutes seconds|35|37|10.41|N|137|13|40.00|E|scale:20000}} | Enshrined deities = {{ill|lt=Kikuri-hime no Kami|Q11619891|WD=Q11619891|1=Kukurihime}}　{{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813858|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi-no-Mikoto|1=Izanagi}}　{{ill|lt=Izanami|Q682306|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami-no-Mikoto|1=Izanami}} | Shrine rank = Former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}, disputed {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, {{ill|lt=Kinpei-sha|Q119929592|WD=Q119929592|1=Kinpei-sha}} | Founded = (Traditionally) Year 2 of {{ill|lt=Yōrō|Q1373061|WD=Q1373061|1=Yōrō}} ({{ill|lt=718|Q23534|WD=Q23534|1=718}}) | Main hall = | Annual festivals = {{ill|lt=Vernal Equinox Day|Q1049141|WD=Q1049141|1=Vernal Equinox Day}} (Spring Grand Festival)　November 23 (Autumn Grand Festival) | Rituals = | Map = {{Location map|Japan Gifu|label=Oyama
Hakusan Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=230}} }} '''Oyama Hakusan Shrine''' (おおやまはくさんじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|lt=Shirakawa Town|Q1203350|WD=Q1203350|1=Shirakawa, Gifu (town)}}, {{ill|lt=Kamo District|Q1348656|WD=Q1348656|1=Kamo District, Gifu}}, {{ill|lt=Gifu Prefecture|Q131277|WD=Q131277|1=Gifu Prefecture}}. It stands on the summit of Mount Hakusan (elevation 862 m). Its former {{ill|lt=shrine rank|Q10444029|WD=Q10444029|1=Shakaku}} was Prefectural Shrine. There is a theory identifying it with the Oyama Shrine of Kamo District in {{ill|lt=Mino Province|Q864156|WD=Q864156|1=Mino Province}} listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}. The current shrine buildings were rebuilt after the {{ill|lt=Kansei era|Q1195131|WD=Q1195131|1=Kansei}}. === Enshrined Deities === === Principal Deities === * {{ill|lt=Kikuri-hime no Kami|Q11619891|WD=Q11619891|1=Kukurihime}} (Hakusan Hime no Kami / {{ill|lt=Hakusan Gongen|Q11579578|WD=Q11579578|1=Hakusan Gongen}}) * {{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813858|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi-no-Mikoto|1=Izanagi}} * {{ill|lt=Izanami|Q682306|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami-no-Mikoto|1=Izanami}} === Deities of Subsidiary Shrines === * {{ill|lt=Kumano Gongen|Q11568925|WD=Q11568925|lt=Ketsumiko no Kami|1=Kumano Gongen}} * {{ill|lt=Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Q335618|WD=Q335618|lt=Hayatamao no Kami|1=Kumano Hayatama Taisha}} * {{ill|lt=Kumano Gongen|Q11568925|WD=Q11568925|lt=Kumano Fusumi no Kami|1=Kumano Gongen}} * {{ill|lt=Shinatsuhiko|Q1073925|WD=Q1073925|lt=Shinatsuhiko no Kami|1=Shinatsuhiko}} === Annual Festivals === === Spring Grand Festival === Held on {{ill|lt=Vernal Equinox Day|Q1049141|WD=Q1049141|1=Vernal Equinox Day}} in March. During the Spring Grand Festival, an offering envoy is dispatched from the Gifu Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines. After the ritual prayerA prayer recited by the offering envoy at regular festivals, enshrinement festivals, main hall relocation ceremonies, and periodic festivals at shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines. is offered, shrine maidens perform the Otome no Mai and Urayasu no Mai, and a lion dance (Shishi Kagura) is dedicated. === Autumn Grand Festival (Niiname-sai) === Held on {{ill|lt=Labor Thanksgiving Day|Q82405|WD=Q82405|1=Labor Thanksgiving Day}} in November. After the ritual prayer is recited, shrine maidens perform the Otome no Mai, Urayasu no Mai, and Shishi Kagura, and mochi-throwing is also held outside the worship hall. On the same day, {{ill|lt=Shichi-Go-San|Q1072387|WD=Q1072387|1=Shichi-Go-San}} prayers are also conducted. === History === According to tradition, in the 2nd year of {{ill|lt=Yōrō|Q1373061|WD=Q1373061|1=Yōrō}} ({{ill|lt=718|Q23534|WD=Q23534|1=718}}), {{ill|lt=Taichō|Q2388439|WD=Q2388439|1=Taichō}} transferred the deity from {{ill|lt=Hakusan Hime Shrine|Q704702|WD=Q704702|1=Shirayama Hime Shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Kaga Province|Q907469|WD=Q907469|1=Kaga Province}} and founded the shrine. One theory holds that it was one of the “Seven Hakusan Shrines of Hokuriku” established by Taichō in the same yearAnother of the Seven Hakusan Shrines of Hokuriku is {{ill|lt=Nōgō Hakusan Shrine|Q11612076|WD=Q11612076|1=Nōgō Hakusan Shrine}}.. It is said that when the deity was transferred, it was first enshrined via Mount Suishō (a 687 m mountain near the borders of Shirakawa Town, {{ill|lt=Hichisō Town|Q1203839|WD=Q1203839|1=Hichisō, Gifu}}, and {{ill|lt=Gero City|Q819639|WD=Q819639|1=Gero, Gifu}}). Since ancient times, it served as the chief guardian shrine of Shirakawa and Sami-go, with residents of 36 nearby villages as parishioners. In the 12th year of {{ill|lt=Bunmei (Japan)|Q1193544|WD=Q1193544|lt=Bunmei|1=Bunmei}} ({{ill|lt=1480|Q6628|WD=Q6628|1=1480}}), it was restored by '''Yasue Nakatsukasa-no-jō Mitsuami Motomasa''', lord of Nohara Castle. From the medieval period, the syncretic Shinto-Buddhist temple {{ill|lt=Muromatsu-ji (Shirakawa Town, Gifu Prefecture)|UNKNOWN|lt=Muromatsu-ji}} of {{ill|lt=Shugendō|Q1483875|WD=Q1483875|1=Shugendō}} served as the {{ill|lt=bettō-ji|Q11396755|WD=Q11396755|1=Bettō-ji}} (administrative temple) and conducted rituals. According to a provenance record of '''Oyama Hakusan Gongen''' dated September 18, the 6th year of {{ill|lt=Tenbun era|Q1192853|WD=Q1192853|lt=Tenbun|1=Tenbun}} ({{ill|lt=1537|Q6461|WD=Q6461|1=1537}}), signed and sealed by the Muromatsu-ji bettō '''Shamon Enkai''': {{Quote|The main hall represents the true Buddhist nature of Hakusan, the Eleven-faced Kannon, and is eleven bays square. The five principal icons in the inner sanctuary are: the central icon, {{ill|lt=Eleven-faced Kannon|Q11404731|WD=Q11404731|1=Ekādaśamukha}}; to the left and right, {{ill|lt=Thousand-armed Kannon|Q10904910|WD=Q10904910|1=Thousand-armed Kannon}}; the teacher of the Western Paradise, {{ill|lt=Maitreya|UNKNOWN|lt=Miroku Buddha}}; and the savior of the six realms, {{ill|lt=Jizō Bosatsu|Q604687|WD=Q604687|1=Kṣitigarbha}}. All of these were made by the Great Master Taichō. In the outer sanctuary are placed {{ill|lt=Fudō Myōō|UNKNOWN|lt=Fudō}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Bishamonten|1=UNKNOWN}}. Ascending to the inner sanctuary of Ōmine, there are three shrines: the central one is Hakusan Gongen, and the two side subsidiary shrines are Kumano and {{ill|lt=Suwa Shrine|Q11556504|WD=Q11556504|lt=Suwa Gongen|1=Suhara Shrine}}. Also, in the treasure hall {{ill|lt=Daikokuten|Q1759916|WD=Q1759916|1=Daikokuten}} is enshrined. Next, the principal icon of the Goma Hall of the bettō temple Muromatsu-ji is called the principal icon of Mount Betsu, a seated statue of Shō-Kannon Bosatsu, over four shaku in height, made by {{ill|lt=Gyōki|Q1136664|WD=Q1136664|1=Gyōki}} Bosatsu, the founder of this temple.}} It is noted at the end that although such origins of the mountain had existed since ancient times, some portions had become illegible due to insect damage. In August of the 4th year of {{ill|lt=Genki|Q1145064|WD=Q1145064|1=Genki (era)}} (the 1st year of {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}}) ({{ill|lt=1573|Q6678|WD=Q6678|1=1573}}), Nohara Castle fell to {{ill|lt=Tōyama Tomotada|Q11642011|WD=Q11642011|1=Tōyama Tomotada}}. The castle lord '''Yasue Sanai Masatomo''' fled to {{ill|lt=Bingo Province|Q1049113|WD=Q1049113|1=Bingo Province}}, while his younger brother '''Yasue Koyoemon Masanori''' eventually fled to {{ill|lt=Bungo Province|Q864148|WD=Q864148|1=Bungo Province}}, and their local power base was lost. In the 10th year of {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} ({{ill|lt=1582|Q6719|WD=Q6719|1=1582}}), Nohara Castle and Koshihara Castle fell after an attack by {{ill|lt=Mori Nagayoshi|Q1071391|WD=Q1071391|1=Mori Nagayoshi}}, lord of {{ill|lt=Kanayama Castle (Mino Province)|Q11646975|WD=Q11646975|lt=Kanayama Castle|1=Kaneyama Castle}}. The {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yasue clan|1=UNKNOWN}} thereafter abandoned the warrior class and returned to farming; others served the {{ill|lt=Asano clan|Q2659895|WD=Q2659895|1=Asano clan}} or became rural samurai. From then on, generation after generation, the Yasue clan took responsibility for the repair of Oyama Hakusan Shrine. During the Edo period, the area belonged to {{ill|lt=Naegi Domain|Q10863458|WD=Q10863458|1=Naegi Domain}}, but because parishioner villages were also under {{ill|lt=Tenryō|Q1685411|WD=Q1685411|lt=shogunate lands|1=Tenryō}} and {{ill|lt=Owari Domain|Q1062072|WD=Q1062072|1=Owari Domain}}, rice and money were donated by each magistrate whenever repairs were carried out. On September 15, the 1st year of {{ill|lt=Kan’en|Q1145723|WD=Q1145723|1=Kan'en}} ({{ill|lt=1748|Q7600|WD=Q7600|1=1748}}), the administrative head of {{ill|lt=Sanbō-in|Q3471686|WD=Q3471686|1=Sanbō-in}} in Kyoto Daigo granted the divine rank of {{ill|lt=Senior First Rank|Q11123258|WD=Q11123258|1=Senior First Rank}} to “Mino Province Oyama Hakusan Daigongen.” The construction from the 5th year of {{ill|lt=Kansei|Q1195131|WD=Q1195131|1=Kansei}} ({{ill|lt=1793|Q7807|WD=Q7807|1=1793}}) to the 12th year ({{ill|lt=1800|Q6875|WD=Q6875|1=1800}}), which took eight years, was the largest in scale and involved enormous expense. Because all residents of the foothill villages were parishioners, they cooperated across shogunate, Owari Domain, and Naegi Domain territories to contribute to the construction. The most important site supervisor was '''Takagi Kasuke''', headman of Izumi Village, and the chief accountant was '''Sugiyama Tasaburō''', headman of Yui Village. Donations from the rulers of the parishioner villages were as follows.{{Quotation|One: 50 bales of rice — Lord of Naegi Castle, {{ill|lt=Tōyama Tomohisa|Q11642009|WD=Q11642009|lt=Tōyama Sakichi|1=Tōyama Tomohisa}} One: 200 hiki of gold — {{ill|lt=Hida district magistrate|Q11665921|WD=Q11665921|1=Hida gundai}} Iizuka Ihe’e One: 200 hiki of gold — Owari {{ill|lt=Ōta Magistrate’s Office|Q109366193|WD=Q109366193|lt=Ōta magistrate|1=Ōta magistrate}} Nagasaka Ogisuke One: 200 hiki of gold — Owari {{ill|lt=Nishikori River Timber Magistrate’s Office|Q132154387|WD=Q132154387|lt=Nishikori magistrate|1=Nishikori magistrate}} Yao Kichisuke}} In the 4th year of {{ill|lt=Keiō|Q776775|WD=Q776775|1=Keiō}} ({{ill|lt=1868|Q7717|WD=Q7717|1=1868}}), with the enforcement of the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shinbutsu Bunri Edict|1=UNKNOWN}}, the bettō temple Muromatsu-ji was abolished and demolished. The bettō '''Eshitsu''' returned to lay life, changed his name to '''Muromatsu Mino (Muromatsu Yoshio)''', became a Shinto priest, and thereafter devoted himself to the promotion of Shinto. Also, a directive from {{ill|lt=Kasamatsu Prefecture|Q11599927|WD=Q11599927|1=Kasamatsu Prefecture}} noted the discrepancy in the shrine’s name “Mino Province Oyama Hakusan Daigongen,” and it was renamed Hakusan Shrine. The plaque reading “Mino Province Oyama Hakusan Daigongen” is currently preserved in the treasure storehouse. In the 6th year of Meiji ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}), it became a {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} for the Nishi-Shirakawa and Shimo-Sami districts. In the 15th year of Meiji ({{ill|lt=1882|Q7812|WD=Q7812|1=1882}}), the Yasue Kōan Shrine, enshrining Yasue Motomasa, was established as a precinct shrine. In the 39th year of Meiji ({{ill|lt=1906|Q2049|WD=Q2049|1=1906}}), the government implemented the shrine consolidation policy of one shrine per village, and subsidiary shrines were gathered within the precincts. In the 7th year of {{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}} ({{ill|lt=1918|Q2094|WD=Q2094|1=1918}}), it became a Prefectural Shrine. Thereafter, it was designated by the Governor of Gifu Prefecture as a {{ill|lt=shinsen-heihaku-ryō kyōshinsha|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=shrine receiving offerings of sacred food and silk|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} and was revered as a shrine protecting martial fortune. On March 1, {{ill|lt=1953|Q18585|WD=Q18585|1=1953}} (Shōwa 28), it was designated by the Gifu Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines as a Prefectural Shrine Association Head’s Visitation Shrine ({{ill|lt=Kinpei-sha|Q119929592|WD=Q119929592|1=Kinpei-sha}}) (Designation No. 19) {{sfn|Kimura|1997|p=123}}. === Painted Ceiling === In the 11th year of {{ill|lt=Kansei|Q1195131|WD=Q1195131|1=Kansei}} ({{ill|lt=1799|Q7827|WD=Q7827|1=1799}}), 32 Japanese-style color paintings of flowers, trees, animals, and people, painted by the young monk '''Mayuge''' of {{ill|lt=Dōun-ji (Shirakawa Town, Gifu Prefecture)|Q11555882|WD=Q11555882|lt=Dōun-ji|1=Tō'un-ji Temple}}, were affixed to the coffered ceiling of the main hall. They are famous as the “Painted Ceiling of Hakusan Shrine.” Mayuge is said to have been a genius not only in calligraphy and painting but also in scholarship. He moved to Edo to train at Kichijō-ji, but it is said that he was poisoned to death out of jealousy by his peers due to his extraordinary talent. === Precinct Shrines === === Yasue Kōan Shrine === The precinct shrine is separate from the subsidiary shrines. Around the time of the {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707|1=Meiji Restoration}}, there was a village group leader in Utsuo Village named '''Yasue Tarōshichi'''. Tarōshichi’s political career ended in the 6th year of Meiji ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}), and as a remembrance in his later years, he conceived the founding of Yasue Kōan Shrine, traveling far and wide. In the 15th year of Meiji ({{ill|lt=1882|Q7812|WD=Q7812|1=1882}}), the shrine was established within the precincts, enshrining Yasue Nakatsukasa-no-jō Mitsuami Motomasa as its deity. Tarōshichi’s diary contains the following entry: {{Quotation|One: Kamo District, Nohara Castle, Yasue Nakatsukasa-no-jō, Taira no Motomasa, during the Bunmei era, restored the Shirakawa-go village shrine Hakusan Shrine; by the 18th year of Meiji, approximately over 400 years had passed. Accordingly, the hall of Yasue Motomasa was rebuilt and his divine spirit enshrined. November 22, Meiji 18 Shirakawa Village — Organizer: Yasue Tarōshichi Mitono Village — Organizer: Carpenter Yasue Tannemon Shirakawa Village, Muwata — Yasue Tahei Shirakawa Village, Yui — Yasue Yasushi One: 24 yen 35 sen — Hall construction expenses One: 29 yen 35 sen 2 rin — Festival-related expenses One: 3 yen — Fence expenses Total: 56 yen 70 sen 2 rin Of which: 39 yen 21 sen rin — Voluntary donations 3 yen 53 sen 6 rin — Yasue Tahei 7 yen 7 sen 4 rin — Yasue Tarōshichi 2 yen 50 sen — Yasue Yasushi 1 yen — Yasue Tannemon 50 sen — Izumi, Yasue Kitarō 50 sen — Arimoto, Yasue Yūtarō 2 yen 33 sen 6 rin — Yasue Tarōshichi (Value of white rice: 4 to 9 shō 3 gō)}} === Designated Cultural Properties === === Nationally Designated Natural Monument === * Great Cedar of Oyama (National {{ill|lt=Natural Monument|Q23790|WD=Q23790|1=Natural monument}}){{Cite web|Japanese|date= |url=http://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazai/17768/tennen/ooyama.html |title=Great Cedar of Oyama |publisher=Gifu Prefecture |accessdate=2017-09-17}} On the right side of the worship hall, midway up the stone steps leading to the main hall, stands a giant cedar designated as a National Natural Monument, said to be over 1,200 years old, with a circumference of 8.9 m and a height of 36 m. Because the area around the shrine forms a well-preserved green space mainly consisting of {{ill|lt=Japanese cedar|Q147388|WD=Q147388|1=Cryptomeria}} and {{ill|lt=Japanese cypress|Q917187|WD=Q917187|1=Chamaecyparis obtusa}}, it was designated as a Green Environment Conservation Area on November 6, {{ill|lt=1984|Q2432|WD=Q2432|1=1984}} (Shōwa 59){{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/page/6062.html|title=List of Green Environment Conservation Areas|publisher=Gifu Prefecture|date=|accessdate=2024-10-20}}. === Access === ; Public Transportation * {{ill|lt=Central Japan Railway Company|Q513679|WD=Q513679|lt=JR|1=Central Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|lt=Takayama Main Line|Q1188357|WD=Q1188357|1=Takayama Main Line}} — Get off at {{ill|lt=Shirakawa-guchi Station|Q5358687|WD=Q5358687|1=Shirakawaguchi Station}} or {{ill|lt=Shimoyui Station|Q7497027|WD=Q7497027|1=Shimoyui Station}}. Approximately 15 km from Shirakawa-guchi Station, and about 10 km from Shimoyui Station. === Notes === {{Footnote help}} {{Reflist}} === References === * “Shirakawa Town History,” Volume 3: Life and Culture, Chapter 5: Religion, Section 2: Shrines Enshrined in Various Areas, 1. Former Nishi-Shirakawa Village, Mitono aza Oyama, Hakusan Shrine, pp. 839–842, Shirakawa Town History Editorial Committee, 1968 * “Shirakawa Town History,” Volume 3: Life and Culture, Chapter 5: Religion, Section 4: Temples, 2. Former Nishi-Shirakawa Village, Kyoto Sanbō-in branch temple, Shirakawa-san Muromatsu-ji, pp. 866–870, Shirakawa Town History Editorial Committee, 1968 * {{Cite book |Japanese |editor=Kimura Akira |title=Tour of Major Shrines of Gifu Prefecture |publisher={{ill|lt=Nagara Tenjin Shrine|Q3335060|WD=Q3335060|1=Nagara Tenjin Shrine}} |date=1997 |ref={{SfnRef|Kimura|1997}}}} === External Links === * [https://www.gifu-jinjacho.jp/syosai.php?shrno=495&shrname=%E2%98%85%E5%A4%A7%E5%B1%B1%E7%99%BD%E5%B1%B1%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE%E2%98%85 Oyama Hakusan Shrine – Gifu Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines] * [https://mino-shirakawa.com/explore/ooyamahakusannjinjya/ Oyama Hakusan Shrine – Shirakawa Town Tourism Association] * [https://mino-shirakawa.com/event/ooyamahakusannjinnjyasyunnkitaisai/ Oyama Hakusan Shrine Spring Grand Festival – Shirakawa Town Tourism Association] * [https://mino-shirakawa.com/event/ooyamahakusaooyamahakusannjinjya/ Oyama Hakusan Shrine Autumn Grand Festival (Niiname-sai) – Shirakawa Town Tourism Association] * [https://mino-shirakawa.com/explore/ooyamahakusanjinjyaoosugi/ Oyama Hakusan Shrine Great Cedar – Shirakawa Town Tourism Association] == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Ōyama Hakusan Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Ōyama Hakusan Shrine]][[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Mino Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Gifu Prefecture]] [[Category:白川町の建築物]] [[Category:白川町の歴史]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Mino Province]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Gifu Prefecture]] [[Category:白山神社]]

Hakusan Shrine]]
Shrine buildings | Location = {{ill|lt=Gifu Prefecture|Q131277|WD=Q131277|1=Gifu Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Kamo District|Q1348656|WD=Q1348656|1=Kamo District, Gifu}}{{ill|lt=Shirakawa Town|Q1203350|WD=Q1203350|1=Shirakawa, Gifu (town)}} Mitono 1096 | Coordinates = {{Wiki coordinates 2-step degrees minutes seconds|35|37|10.41|N|137|13|40.00|E|scale:20000}} | Enshrined deities = {{ill|lt=Kikuri-hime no Kami|Q11619891|WD=Q11619891|1=Kikurihime}}　{{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813858|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi-no-Mikoto|1=Izanagi}}　{{ill|lt=Izanami|Q682306|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami-no-Mikoto|1=Izanami}} | Shrine rank = Former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}, disputed {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, {{ill|lt=Kinpei-sha|Q119929592|WD=Q119929592|1=Kinpei-sha}} | Founded = (Traditionally) Year 2 of {{ill|lt=Yōrō|Q1373061|WD=Q1373061|1=Yōrō}} ({{ill|lt=718|Q23534|WD=Q23534|1=718}}) | Main hall = | Annual festivals = {{ill|lt=Vernal Equinox Day|Q1049141|WD=Q1049141|1=Vernal Equinox Day}} (Spring Grand Festival)　November 23 (Autumn Grand Festival) | Rituals = | Map = {{Location map|Japan Gifu|label=Oyama
Hakusan Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=230}} }} '''Oyama Hakusan Shrine''' (おおやまはくさんじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|lt=Shirakawa Town|Q1203350|WD=Q1203350|1=Shirakawa, Gifu (town)}}, {{ill|lt=Kamo District|Q1348656|WD=Q1348656|1=Kamo District, Gifu}}, {{ill|lt=Gifu Prefecture|Q131277|WD=Q131277|1=Gifu Prefecture}}. It stands on the summit of Mount Hakusan (elevation 862 m). Its former {{ill|lt=shrine rank|Q10444029|WD=Q10444029|1=Shakaku}} was Prefectural Shrine. There is a theory identifying it with the Oyama Shrine of Kamo District in {{ill|lt=Mino Province|Q864156|WD=Q864156|1=Mino Province}} listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}. The current shrine buildings were rebuilt after the {{ill|lt=Kansei era|Q1195131|WD=Q1195131|1=Kansei}}. === Enshrined Deities === === Principal Deities === * {{ill|lt=Kikuri-hime no Kami|Q11619891|WD=Q11619891|1=Kikurihime}} (Hakusan Hime no Kami / {{ill|lt=Hakusan Gongen|Q11579578|WD=Q11579578|1=Hakusan Gongen}}) * {{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813858|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi-no-Mikoto|1=Izanagi}} * {{ill|lt=Izanami|Q682306|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami-no-Mikoto|1=Izanami}} === Deities of Subsidiary Shrines === * {{ill|lt=Kumano Gongen|Q11568925|WD=Q11568925|lt=Ketsumiko no Kami|1=Kumano Gongen}} * {{ill|lt=Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Q335618|WD=Q335618|lt=Hayatamao no Kami|1=Kumano Hayatama Taisha}} * {{ill|lt=Kumano Gongen|Q11568925|WD=Q11568925|lt=Kumano Fusumi no Kami|1=Kumano Gongen}} * {{ill|lt=Shinatsuhiko|Q1073925|WD=Q1073925|lt=Shinatsuhiko no Kami|1=Shinatsuhiko}} === Annual Festivals === === Spring Grand Festival === Held on {{ill|lt=Vernal Equinox Day|Q1049141|WD=Q1049141|1=Vernal Equinox Day}} in March. During the Spring Grand Festival, an offering envoy is dispatched from the Gifu Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines. After the ritual prayerA prayer recited by the offering envoy at regular festivals, enshrinement festivals, main hall relocation ceremonies, and periodic festivals at shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines. is offered, shrine maidens perform the Otome no Mai and Urayasu no Mai, and a lion dance (Shishi Kagura) is dedicated. === Autumn Grand Festival (Niiname-sai) === Held on {{ill|lt=Labor Thanksgiving Day|Q82405|WD=Q82405|1=Labor Thanksgiving Day}} in November. After the ritual prayer is recited, shrine maidens perform the Otome no Mai, Urayasu no Mai, and Shishi Kagura, and mochi-throwing is also held outside the worship hall. On the same day, {{ill|lt=Shichi-Go-San|Q1072387|WD=Q1072387|1=Shichi-Go-San}} prayers are also conducted. === History === According to tradition, in the 2nd year of {{ill|lt=Yōrō|Q1373061|WD=Q1373061|1=Yōrō}} ({{ill|lt=718|Q23534|WD=Q23534|1=718}}), {{ill|lt=Taichō|Q2388439|WD=Q2388439|1=Taichō}} transferred the deity from {{ill|lt=Hakusan Hime Shrine|Q704702|WD=Q704702|1=Shirayama Hime Shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Kaga Province|Q907469|WD=Q907469|1=Kaga Province}} and founded the shrine. One theory holds that it was one of the “Seven Hakusan Shrines of Hokuriku” established by Taichō in the same yearAnother of the Seven Hakusan Shrines of Hokuriku is {{ill|lt=Nōgō Hakusan Shrine|Q11612076|WD=Q11612076|1=Nōgō Hakusan Shrine}}.. It is said that when the deity was transferred, it was first enshrined via Mount Suishō (a 687 m mountain near the borders of Shirakawa Town, {{ill|lt=Hichisō Town|Q1203839|WD=Q1203839|1=Hichisō, Gifu}}, and {{ill|lt=Gero City|Q819639|WD=Q819639|1=Gero, Gifu}}). Since ancient times, it served as the chief guardian shrine of Shirakawa and Sami-go, with residents of 36 nearby villages as parishioners. In the 12th year of {{ill|lt=Bunmei (Japan)|Q1193544|WD=Q1193544|lt=Bunmei|1=Bunmei}} ({{ill|lt=1480|Q6628|WD=Q6628|1=1480}}), it was restored by '''Yasue Nakatsukasa-no-jō Mitsuami Motomasa''', lord of Nohara Castle. From the medieval period, the syncretic Shinto-Buddhist temple {{ill|lt=Muromatsu-ji (Shirakawa Town, Gifu Prefecture)|UNKNOWN|lt=Muromatsu-ji}} of {{ill|lt=Shugendō|Q1483875|WD=Q1483875|1=Shugendō}} served as the {{ill|lt=bettō-ji|Q11396755|WD=Q11396755|1=Bettō-ji}} (administrative temple) and conducted rituals. According to a provenance record of '''Oyama Hakusan Gongen''' dated September 18, the 6th year of {{ill|lt=Tenbun era|Q1192853|WD=Q1192853|lt=Tenbun|1=Tenbun}} ({{ill|lt=1537|Q6461|WD=Q6461|1=1537}}), signed and sealed by the Muromatsu-ji bettō '''Shamon Enkai''': {{Quote|The main hall represents the true Buddhist nature of Hakusan, the Eleven-faced Kannon, and is eleven bays square. The five principal icons in the inner sanctuary are: the central icon, {{ill|lt=Eleven-faced Kannon|Q11404731|WD=Q11404731|1=Ekādaśamukha}}; to the left and right, {{ill|lt=Thousand-armed Kannon|Q10904910|WD=Q10904910|1=Thousand-armed Kannon}}; the teacher of the Western Paradise, {{ill|lt=Maitreya|UNKNOWN|lt=Miroku Buddha}}; and the savior of the six realms, {{ill|lt=Jizō Bosatsu|Q604687|WD=Q604687|1=Kṣitigarbha}}. All of these were made by the Great Master Taichō. In the outer sanctuary are placed {{ill|lt=Fudō Myōō|UNKNOWN|lt=Fudō}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Bishamonten|1=UNKNOWN}}. Ascending to the inner sanctuary of Ōmine, there are three shrines: the central one is Hakusan Gongen, and the two side subsidiary shrines are Kumano and {{ill|lt=Suwa Shrine|Q11556504|WD=Q11556504|lt=Suwa Gongen|1=Suhara Shrine}}. Also, in the treasure hall {{ill|lt=Daikokuten|Q1759916|WD=Q1759916|1=Daikokuten}} is enshrined. Next, the principal icon of the Goma Hall of the bettō temple Muromatsu-ji is called the principal icon of Mount Betsu, a seated statue of Shō-Kannon Bosatsu, over four shaku in height, made by {{ill|lt=Gyōki|Q1136664|WD=Q1136664|1=Gyōki}} Bosatsu, the founder of this temple.}} It is noted at the end that although such origins of the mountain had existed since ancient times, some portions had become illegible due to insect damage. In August of the 4th year of {{ill|lt=Genki|Q1145064|WD=Q1145064|1=Genki (era)}} (the 1st year of {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}}) ({{ill|lt=1573|Q6678|WD=Q6678|1=1573}}), Nohara Castle fell to {{ill|lt=Tōyama Tomotada|Q11642011|WD=Q11642011|1=Tōyama Tomotada}}. The castle lord '''Yasue Sanai Masatomo''' fled to {{ill|lt=Bingo Province|Q1049113|WD=Q1049113|1=Bingo Province}}, while his younger brother '''Yasue Koyoemon Masanori''' eventually fled to {{ill|lt=Bungo Province|Q864148|WD=Q864148|1=Bungo Province}}, and their local power base was lost. In the 10th year of {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} ({{ill|lt=1582|Q6719|WD=Q6719|1=1582}}), Nohara Castle and Koshihara Castle fell after an attack by {{ill|lt=Mori Nagayoshi|Q1071391|WD=Q1071391|1=Mori Nagayoshi}}, lord of {{ill|lt=Kanayama Castle (Mino Province)|Q11646975|WD=Q11646975|lt=Kanayama Castle|1=Kaneyama Castle}}. The {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yasue clan|1=UNKNOWN}} thereafter abandoned the warrior class and returned to farming; others served the {{ill|lt=Asano clan|Q2659895|WD=Q2659895|1=Asano clan}} or became rural samurai. From then on, generation after generation, the Yasue clan took responsibility for the repair of Oyama Hakusan Shrine. During the Edo period, the area belonged to {{ill|lt=Naegi Domain|Q10863458|WD=Q10863458|1=Naegi Domain}}, but because parishioner villages were also under {{ill|lt=Tenryō|Q1685411|WD=Q1685411|lt=shogunate lands|1=Tenryō}} and {{ill|lt=Owari Domain|Q1062072|WD=Q1062072|1=Owari Domain}}, rice and money were donated by each magistrate whenever repairs were carried out. On September 15, the 1st year of {{ill|lt=Kan’en|Q1145723|WD=Q1145723|1=Kan'en}} ({{ill|lt=1748|Q7600|WD=Q7600|1=1748}}), the administrative head of {{ill|lt=Sanbō-in|Q3471686|WD=Q3471686|1=Sanbō-in}} in Kyoto Daigo granted the divine rank of {{ill|lt=Senior First Rank|Q11123258|WD=Q11123258|1=Senior First Rank}} to “Mino Province Oyama Hakusan Daigongen.” The construction from the 5th year of {{ill|lt=Kansei|Q1195131|WD=Q1195131|1=Kansei}} ({{ill|lt=1793|Q7807|WD=Q7807|1=1793}}) to the 12th year ({{ill|lt=1800|Q6875|WD=Q6875|1=1800}}), which took eight years, was the largest in scale and involved enormous expense. Because all residents of the foothill villages were parishioners, they cooperated across shogunate, Owari Domain, and Naegi Domain territories to contribute to the construction. The most important site supervisor was '''Takagi Kasuke''', headman of Izumi Village, and the chief accountant was '''Sugiyama Tasaburō''', headman of Yui Village. Donations from the rulers of the parishioner villages were as follows.{{Quotation|One: 50 bales of rice — Lord of Naegi Castle, {{ill|lt=Tōyama Tomohisa|Q11642009|WD=Q11642009|lt=Tōyama Sakichi|1=Tōyama Tomohisa}} One: 200 hiki of gold — {{ill|lt=Hida district magistrate|Q11665921|WD=Q11665921|1=Hida gundai}} Iizuka Ihe’e One: 200 hiki of gold — Owari {{ill|lt=Ōta Magistrate’s Office|Q109366193|WD=Q109366193|lt=Ōta magistrate|1=Ōta magistrate}} Nagasaka Ogisuke One: 200 hiki of gold — Owari {{ill|lt=Nishikori River Timber Magistrate’s Office|Q132154387|WD=Q132154387|lt=Nishikori magistrate|1=Nishikori magistrate}} Yao Kichisuke}} In the 4th year of {{ill|lt=Keiō|Q776775|WD=Q776775|1=Keiō}} ({{ill|lt=1868|Q7717|WD=Q7717|1=1868}}), with the enforcement of the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shinbutsu Bunri Edict|1=UNKNOWN}}, the bettō temple Muromatsu-ji was abolished and demolished. The bettō '''Eshitsu''' returned to lay life, changed his name to '''Muromatsu Mino (Muromatsu Yoshio)''', became a Shinto priest, and thereafter devoted himself to the promotion of Shinto. Also, a directive from {{ill|lt=Kasamatsu Prefecture|Q11599927|WD=Q11599927|1=Kasamatsu Prefecture}} noted the discrepancy in the shrine’s name “Mino Province Oyama Hakusan Daigongen,” and it was renamed Hakusan Shrine. The plaque reading “Mino Province Oyama Hakusan Daigongen” is currently preserved in the treasure storehouse. In the 6th year of Meiji ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}), it became a {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} for the Nishi-Shirakawa and Shimo-Sami districts. In the 15th year of Meiji ({{ill|lt=1882|Q7812|WD=Q7812|1=1882}}), the Yasue Kōan Shrine, enshrining Yasue Motomasa, was established as a precinct shrine. In the 39th year of Meiji ({{ill|lt=1906|Q2049|WD=Q2049|1=1906}}), the government implemented the shrine consolidation policy of one shrine per village, and subsidiary shrines were gathered within the precincts. In the 7th year of {{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}} ({{ill|lt=1918|Q2094|WD=Q2094|1=1918}}), it became a Prefectural Shrine. Thereafter, it was designated by the Governor of Gifu Prefecture as a {{ill|lt=shinsen-heihaku-ryō kyōshinsha|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=shrine receiving offerings of sacred food and silk|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} and was revered as a shrine protecting martial fortune. On March 1, {{ill|lt=1953|Q18585|WD=Q18585|1=1953}} (Shōwa 28), it was designated by the Gifu Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines as a Prefectural Shrine Association Head’s Visitation Shrine ({{ill|lt=Kinpei-sha|Q119929592|WD=Q119929592|1=Kinpei-sha}}) (Designation No. 19) {{sfn|Kimura|1997|p=123}}. === Painted Ceiling === In the 11th year of {{ill|lt=Kansei|Q1195131|WD=Q1195131|1=Kansei}} ({{ill|lt=1799|Q7827|WD=Q7827|1=1799}}), 32 Japanese-style color paintings of flowers, trees, animals, and people, painted by the young monk '''Mayuge''' of {{ill|lt=Dōun-ji (Shirakawa Town, Gifu Prefecture)|Q11555882|WD=Q11555882|lt=Dōun-ji|1=Tō'un-ji Temple}}, were affixed to the coffered ceiling of the main hall. They are famous as the “Painted Ceiling of Hakusan Shrine.” Mayuge is said to have been a genius not only in calligraphy and painting but also in scholarship. He moved to Edo to train at Kichijō-ji, but it is said that he was poisoned to death out of jealousy by his peers due to his extraordinary talent. === Precinct Shrines === === Yasue Kōan Shrine === The precinct shrine is separate from the subsidiary shrines. Around the time of the {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707|1=Meiji Restoration}}, there was a village group leader in Utsuo Village named '''Yasue Tarōshichi'''. Tarōshichi’s political career ended in the 6th year of Meiji ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}), and as a remembrance in his later years, he conceived the founding of Yasue Kōan Shrine, traveling far and wide. In the 15th year of Meiji ({{ill|lt=1882|Q7812|WD=Q7812|1=1882}}), the shrine was established within the precincts, enshrining Yasue Nakatsukasa-no-jō Mitsuami Motomasa as its deity. Tarōshichi’s diary contains the following entry: {{Quotation|One: Kamo District, Nohara Castle, Yasue Nakatsukasa-no-jō, Taira no Motomasa, during the Bunmei era, restored the Shirakawa-go village shrine Hakusan Shrine; by the 18th year of Meiji, approximately over 400 years had passed. Accordingly, the hall of Yasue Motomasa was rebuilt and his divine spirit enshrined. November 22, Meiji 18 Shirakawa Village — Organizer: Yasue Tarōshichi Mitono Village — Organizer: Carpenter Yasue Tannemon Shirakawa Village, Muwata — Yasue Tahei Shirakawa Village, Yui — Yasue Yasushi One: 24 yen 35 sen — Hall construction expenses One: 29 yen 35 sen 2 rin — Festival-related expenses One: 3 yen — Fence expenses Total: 56 yen 70 sen 2 rin Of which: 39 yen 21 sen rin — Voluntary donations 3 yen 53 sen 6 rin — Yasue Tahei 7 yen 7 sen 4 rin — Yasue Tarōshichi 2 yen 50 sen — Yasue Yasushi 1 yen — Yasue Tannemon 50 sen — Izumi, Yasue Kitarō 50 sen — Arimoto, Yasue Yūtarō 2 yen 33 sen 6 rin — Yasue Tarōshichi (Value of white rice: 4 to 9 shō 3 gō)}} === Designated Cultural Properties === === Nationally Designated Natural Monument === * Great Cedar of Oyama (National {{ill|lt=Natural Monument|Q23790|WD=Q23790|1=Natural monument}}){{Cite web|Japanese|date= |url=http://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazai/17768/tennen/ooyama.html |title=Great Cedar of Oyama |publisher=Gifu Prefecture |accessdate=2017-09-17}} On the right side of the worship hall, midway up the stone steps leading to the main hall, stands a giant cedar designated as a National Natural Monument, said to be over 1,200 years old, with a circumference of 8.9 m and a height of 36 m. Because the area around the shrine forms a well-preserved green space mainly consisting of {{ill|lt=Japanese cedar|Q147388|WD=Q147388|1=Cryptomeria}} and {{ill|lt=Japanese cypress|Q917187|WD=Q917187|1=Chamaecyparis obtusa}}, it was designated as a Green Environment Conservation Area on November 6, {{ill|lt=1984|Q2432|WD=Q2432|1=1984}} (Shōwa 59){{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/page/6062.html|title=List of Green Environment Conservation Areas|publisher=Gifu Prefecture|date=|accessdate=2024-10-20}}. === Access === ; Public Transportation * {{ill|lt=Central Japan Railway Company|Q513679|WD=Q513679|lt=JR|1=Central Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|lt=Takayama Main Line|Q1188357|WD=Q1188357|1=Takayama Main Line}} — Get off at {{ill|lt=Shirakawa-guchi Station|Q5358687|WD=Q5358687|1=Shirakawaguchi Station}} or {{ill|lt=Shimoyui Station|Q7497027|WD=Q7497027|1=Shimoyui Station}}. Approximately 15 km from Shirakawa-guchi Station, and about 10 km from Shimoyui Station. === Notes === {{Footnote help}} {{Reflist}} === References === * “Shirakawa Town History,” Volume 3: Life and Culture, Chapter 5: Religion, Section 2: Shrines Enshrined in Various Areas, 1. Former Nishi-Shirakawa Village, Mitono aza Oyama, Hakusan Shrine, pp. 839–842, Shirakawa Town History Editorial Committee, 1968 * “Shirakawa Town History,” Volume 3: Life and Culture, Chapter 5: Religion, Section 4: Temples, 2. Former Nishi-Shirakawa Village, Kyoto Sanbō-in branch temple, Shirakawa-san Muromatsu-ji, pp. 866–870, Shirakawa Town History Editorial Committee, 1968 * {{Cite book |Japanese |editor=Kimura Akira |title=Tour of Major Shrines of Gifu Prefecture |publisher={{ill|lt=Nagara Tenjin Shrine|Q3335060|WD=Q3335060|1=Nagara Tenjin Shrine}} |date=1997 |ref={{SfnRef|Kimura|1997}}}} === External Links === * [https://www.gifu-jinjacho.jp/syosai.php?shrno=495&shrname=%E2%98%85%E5%A4%A7%E5%B1%B1%E7%99%BD%E5%B1%B1%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE%E2%98%85 Oyama Hakusan Shrine – Gifu Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines] * [https://mino-shirakawa.com/explore/ooyamahakusannjinjya/ Oyama Hakusan Shrine – Shirakawa Town Tourism Association] * [https://mino-shirakawa.com/event/ooyamahakusannjinnjyasyunnkitaisai/ Oyama Hakusan Shrine Spring Grand Festival – Shirakawa Town Tourism Association] * [https://mino-shirakawa.com/event/ooyamahakusaooyamahakusannjinjya/ Oyama Hakusan Shrine Autumn Grand Festival (Niiname-sai) – Shirakawa Town Tourism Association] * [https://mino-shirakawa.com/explore/ooyamahakusanjinjyaoosugi/ Oyama Hakusan Shrine Great Cedar – Shirakawa Town Tourism Association] == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Ōyama Hakusan Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Ōyama Hakusan Shrine]][[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Mino Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Gifu Prefecture]] [[Category:白川町の建築物]] [[Category:白川町の歴史]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Mino Province]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Gifu Prefecture]] [[Category:白山神社]]
